How Procurement Impacts on Profitability/Savings
Annual sales turnover
Annual spend
Profit
Profit as a percentage of sales
Purchased goods and services account for 50% of the cost of sales with the balance being the cost of overheads.
Assume cost of purchases has been reduced by £500k or 2%
Annual sales turnover
Annual spend
Profit
Profit as a percentage of sales

Private sector - A 2% reduction in purchases has increased profits by 10% (existing profits £5m now £5.5 million)
Public sector - A 2% reduction in purchases has increased additional spend for other/additional areas by 10% (Increased VFM as a % total spend)
NAO - 3 E’s (now 4 E’s) Economy - Efficiency - Effectiveness…………Equity
https://www.nao.org.uk/successful-commissioning/general-principles/value-for-money/assessing-value-for-money/#
How Procurement Impacts on Profitability/Savings
Source: Andrew Cox et al:  SCM A Guide to Best Practice (2003)

Consider your organisation:
Are all savings about the reduced cost of a product or service?
What other opportunities (apart from cost) are there to make savings?
How could they impact on improved profitability and/or reduced spend?
How effective procurement impacts on profitability/savings

The Roles of Procurement and Supply
In this Unit, we will focus on:
The Proportion of Costs Accounted for by Procurement
An example of the importance of procurement is provided by McKinsey when they commented that:

“Procurement is another financial management process that can be optimised to produce rapid savings without diminishing levels of service. Purchased goods and services account for 1/3 of total public spending in OECD countries....Different agencies in the same government often pay vastly different prices for the same things. Fortunately savings can often be realised quickly by introducing better procurement practices”
Source: McKinsey, Better for Less Improving Public Sector Performance on a Tight Budget (2011)
The Proportion of Costs Accounted for by Procurement

It is possible through open book accounting to evaluate the costs of purchases and  to produce a cost model.
This will also help distinguish the proportions of suppliers costs that are accounted for by their purchases
The Proportion of Costs Accounted for by Procurement
Using your organisation, itemise the costs that are not related to procurement and so will be internal to the business.

Salaries/ wages/pension contributions(but not benefits such as healthcare, cars etc.)
Regulatory expenses generally paid to government agencies
Dividends paid to shareholders
Compensation/fines/warranty claims etc., paid to customers
Depreciation charges applied to fixed assets
Taxes paid on the organisations operations such as Business Rates/ National Insurance etc.
Proportions of costs accounted for by procurement & supply

Outsourcing of core and non-core services
The Roles of Procurement and Supply
In this Unit, we will focus on:
Outsourcing
DEFINITION
EXPLANATION
A service contract is a supply contract concerned with sourcing a service rather than a tangible product
Company A wishes to purchase a service and enters into a contract with a selected supplier (Company B) to have the service performed on agreed terms
Subcontracting is the use of an outside organisation to do work that the buying organisation cannot do itself, because of a temporary shortage of resources or lack of capacity

Company A contracts Company B (the main contractor) to perform certain work, such as cleaning services. Company B lacks capacity to perform all the work itself. In order to meet Company A’s requirements, Company B subcontracts some of the work to Company C, if permitted to do so by its contract with Company A
Outsourcing is the strategic contracting out of major work, previously carried out in-house, to an external service provider

The outsourcer (Company A) will draw up a long-term contract specifying the work to be performed and the service levels to be achieved by an outsource provider (Company B). The outsourcer retains responsibility for satisfactory completion of the work, but delegates all day-to-day operations to the outsource provider
Insourcing is the opposite of outsourcing
The organisation previously outsourced the work, but now decides to bring it in-house
Outsourcing Exercise

In your view what do you believe are the key drivers for outsourcing?
What evidence can you provide to support your view

Tutor Note:
Accelerate reengineering benefits
Access to world class capabilities
Cash infusion
Free resources for other purposes
Function difficult to manage or out of control
Improve company focus
Make capital funds available
Reduce operating costs
Reduce risk
Resources not available internally
Discussion point: Is outsourcing as popular as it once was? If not why might attitudes have changed?
Outsourcing

Supports organisational rationalisation and downsizing
Allows focused investment of managerial, staff and other resources on the organisation’s core activities and competencies
Accesses and leverages the specialist expertise, technology and resources of contractors
Access to economies of scale
Adds competitive performance incentives
Leverages collaborative supply relationships, and can support synergies (2 + 2 = 5)
Cost certainty for activities where demand and costs are uncertain or fluctuating
ADVANTAGES:
Outsourcing

Potentially higher cost of services (including contractor profit margin), contracting and management
Difficulty of ensuring service quality and consistency and corporate social responsibility
Potential loss of in-house expertise, knowledge, contacts or technologies in the service area
Potential loss of control over key areas of performance and risk: over-dependence on suppliers
Added distance from the customer or end-user, by having an intermediary service provider
DISADVANTAGES:
Outsourcing

Risks of ‘lock in’ to an incompatible or under-performing relationship: cultural or ethical incompatibility; relationship management difficulties; contractor inflexibility, conflict of interest, complacency or loss of client focus
Risks of loss of control over confidential data and intellectual property
Ethical and employee relations issues of transfer or cessation of activities
Potential risks, costs and difficulties of in-sourcing if the outsource arrangement fails
DISADVANTAGES:
Outsourcing
Adapted from CIPS Study Materials
Outsourcing: The Procurement Process
Feasibility study and business case development
Define the requirement

A cross-functional task force may be used to consider:
What to outsource (or whether the proposed activity is  suitable for outsourcing)
The business case for outsourcing (strategic reasons; cost comparisons of internal and external provision; anticipated benefits and risks/problems; and possible impacts on staff, capital, finance and competitive advantage)

The requirement may be defined in the form of:
Performance or technical specifications; draft service level agreements
Draft contracts and KPIs
A statement of source requirements (SSR), describing the requirement (specifically for the purposes of market testing)
Tutor Note: students could consider how the exercise might be conducted within their own departments
Outsourcing: The Procurement Process
Appraise and pre-qualify suppliers (where permitted)
Invite tenders against specification

Potential suppliers identified by the buyer (or responding to the SSR) are screened and shortlisted (e.g. using a pre-qualification questionnaire); appraised and pre-qualified (e.g. using dialogue, reference checking, site visits, financial appraisal etc.)
Pre-qualified shortlisted suppliers are formally invited to tender against the tender specification and notified criteria for contract award
Outsourcing: The Procurement Process
Evaluate tenders against specified criteria
Engage in post-tender negotiation
Using best practice tender procedures, tenders are opened and evaluated against pre-notified selection criteria
Post-tender contract negotiation should be used to develop appropriate express contract terms, service performance measures (KPIs) and service level  agreements
Outsourcing: The Procurement Process
Award the contract
Subject to successful conclusion of negotiations, the contract is awarded to the chosen supplier

Contact is maintained between users, providers and contract or SLA managers (on both sides). Service levels are monitored and periodically reviewed, feedback given, and problems dealt with. The SLA itself is periodically reviewed for effectiveness, and adjusted if necessary
Implement contract, performance and relationship management process

The 5 Rights as Business Priorities
The roles and benefits link back to the 5 rights of procurement:
Price
Quality
Quantity
Time
Place
Also identify any conflicts between the 5 Rights and how these might impact, be resolved
